perturbation.original_text: Past due payments under this Agreement shall bear interest at the rate of: (a) 1% per month; or (b) the maximum interest rate permissible under law, whichever is less.
perturbation.changed_text: Past due payments under this Agreement may, at the discretion of the NFLA-NC, bear interest.
perturbation.explanation: This removes the legally required component of charging interest on late payments, contradicting California law regarding interest on debts. By making it discretionary, the contract fails to adhere to the minimum legal requirements.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code Section 3289 specifies the rate of interest chargeable after a breach of contract. Omitting a definite interest rate, or making it discretionary, violates California law related to the enforcement of financial obligations.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3289. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 2. COMPENSATORY RELIEF [3281 - 3361] ( Title 2 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 1. Damages in General [3281 - 3296] ( Chapter 1 enacted 1872. ) ARTICLE 2. Interest as Damages [3287 - 3291] ( Article 2 enacted 1872. ) 3289. (a) Any legal rate of interest stipulated by a contract remains chargeable after a breach thereof, as before, until the contract is superseded by a verdict or other new obligation. (b) If a contract entered into after January 1, 1986, does not stipulate a legal rate of interest, the obligation shall bear interest at a rate of 10 percent per annum after a breach. For the purposes of this subdivision, the term contract shall not include a note secured by a deed of trust on real property. (Amended by Stats. 1986, Ch. 176, Sec. 1. Effective June 23, 1986.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces the original requirement for interest on past due payments with a discretionary option, which contradicts California Civil Code Section 3289. Section 3289(b) states that if a contract does not stipulate a legal rate of interest, the obligation shall bear interest at a rate of 10 percent per annum after a breach. By making interest discretionary, the changed text fails to comply with the legal obligation to charge interest on late payments as outlined in the scraped law snippet. The change removes a legally mandated component, creating a legal loophole, and thus an Omission Legal Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Company agrees to protect, indemnify and hold harmless the NFLA / NFLA-NC and their authorized agents, or any of them, from and against any and all expenses, damages, claims, suits, actions, judgments and costs whatsoever, including reasonable attorney's fees, arising out of, or in any way connected with, actions or omissions of Company, any advertising material furnished by, or an behalf of, Company, or any claim or action for personal injury, death or other cause of action involving alleged defects in Company's Products or services.
perturbation.changed_text: Company agrees to indemnify the NFLA / NFLA-NC and their authorized agents from expenses, damages, claims, suits, actions, judgments and costs arising out of actions or omissions of Company. Attorney's fees are excluded from this indemnification.
perturbation.explanation: By specifically excluding attorney's fees from the indemnification clause, the contract shifts legal costs onto the NFLA/NFLA-NC even when they are not at fault. This could violate California law regarding indemnity, which generally includes attorney's fees as part of the costs to be covered.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code Section 2778 outlines the rules for interpreting indemnity agreements. By excluding attorney's fees, the modified text limits the scope of indemnification in a way that could contradict the implied terms under California law, potentially nullifying or restricting indemnification rights the indemnitee is legally entitled to.

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 4. OBLIGATIONS ARISING FROM PARTICULAR TRANSACTIONS [1738 - 3273.69] ( Part 4 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 12. INDEMNITY [2772 - 2784.5] ( Title 12 enacted 1872. ) 2778. In the interpretation of a contract of indemnity, the following rules are to be applied, unless a contrary intention appears: 1. Upon an indemnity against liability, expressly, or in other equivalent terms, the person indemnified is entitled to recover upon becoming liable; 2. Upon an indemnity against claims, or demands, or damages, or costs, expressly, or in other equivalent terms, the person indemnified is not entitled to recover without payment thereof; 3. An indemnity against claims, or demands, or liability, expressly, or in other equivalent terms, embraces the costs of defense against such claims, demands, or liability incurred in good faith, and in the exercise of a reasonable discretion; 4. The person indemnifying is bound, on request of the person indemnified, to defend actions or proceedings brought against the latter in respect to the matters embraced by the indemnity, but the person indemnified has the right to conduct such defenses, if he chooses to do so; 5. If, after reque
perturbation.justification: The changed text contradicts California law regarding indemnity agreements. The original text includes attorney's fees within the scope of indemnification, but the changed text explicitly excludes them. According to California Civil Code Section 2778, subsection 3, an indemnity against claims or liability embraces the costs of defense against such claims, which includes attorney's fees. By removing attorney's fees, the modified contract limits the scope of indemnification in a way that contradicts the implied terms under California law.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by, and its provisions enforced in accordance with, the laws of California without regard to its principles of conflicts of laws. If a dispute arises under this Agreement which cannot be resolved, such dispute shall be submitted to arbitration and resolved by a single arbitrator (who shall be a lawyer not employed by or associated with either party to this Agreement) in accordance with the Commercial Arbitration Rules of the American Arbitration Association then in effect. All such arbitration shall take place at the office of the American Arbitration Association located within 225 miles of Hayward, California. Each party is entitled to depose one fact witness and all expert witnesses retained by the other party, and to conduct such other discovery as the arbitrator deems appropriate. The award or decision rendered by the arbitrator shall be final, binding and conclusive and judgment may be entered upon such award by any court.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by, and its provisions enforced in accordance with, the laws of California without regard to its principles of conflicts of laws. If a dispute arises under this Agreement which cannot be resolved, such dispute shall be submitted to arbitration and resolved by a single arbitrator. The award or decision rendered by the arbitrator shall be final.
perturbation.explanation: This removes the clause specifying adherence to the Commercial Arbitration Rules of the American Arbitration Association, the location of arbitration and discovery rights. California law requires that arbitration agreements meet certain minimum fairness standards to be enforceable. Removing these provisions could render the arbitration clause unenforceable under California law, particularly regarding discovery rights and fairness.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Code of Civil Procedure Section 1286.2
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure Section 1286.2 outlines grounds for vacating an arbitration award, which includes situations where the arbitrator exceeded their powers or the party's rights were substantially prejudiced. By removing key procedural safeguards, the modified clause significantly increases the risk of an arbitration award being vacated, rendering the arbitration agreement unreliable. Moreover, removing the discovery rights creates an uneven playing field, which could be challenged under California's unconscionability rules for arbitration.

OF SPECIAL PROCEEDINGS OF A CIVIL NATURE [1063 - 1822.60] ( Part 3 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 9. ARBITRATION [1280 - 1294.4] ( Title 9 repealed and added by Stats. 1961, Ch. 461. ) CHAPTER 4. Enforcement of the Award [1285 - 1288.8] ( Chapter 4 added by Stats. 1961, Ch. 461. ) ARTICLE 1. Confirmation, Correction or Vacation of the Award [1285 - 1287.6] ( Article 1 added by Stats. 1961, Ch. 461. ) 1286.2. (a) Subject to Section 1286.4, the court shall vacate the award if the court determines any of the following: (1) The award was procured by corruption, fraud or other undue means. (2) There was corruption in any of the arbitrators. (3) The rights of the party were substantially prejudiced by misconduct of a neutral arbitrator. (4) The arbitrators exceeded their powers and the award cannot be corrected without affecting the merits of the decision upon the controversy submitted. (5) The rights of the party were substantially prejudiced by the refusal of the arbitrators to postpone the hearing upon sufficient cause being shown therefor or by the refusal of the arbitrators to hear evidence material to the controversy or by other conduct of the arbitrators contrary to the provisions of this title. (6) An arbitrator making the award either
perturbation.justification: The modified contract removes key procedural safeguards related to arbitration, including adherence to the Commercial Arbitration Rules of the American Arbitration Association, the location of arbitration, and discovery rights. The original text ensures a fair process through these rules. The removal of these provisions, as explained in the perturbation explanation, increases the risk of an arbitration award being vacated because it removes safeguards, potentially leading to an uneven playing field and unconscionability. The Law Explanation supports this, stating that the modified clause significantly increases the risk of an arbitration award being vacated and removing discovery rights creates an uneven playing field. Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifically CCP 1286.2(a), provides grounds for vacating an arbitration award if the arbitrator exceeded their powers or a party's rights were substantially prejudiced. By omitting the procedural safeguards, the modification creates a contradiction because it weakens the fairness and enforceability of the arbitration agreement under California law.
